nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments where sunlight penetrates; found on reef slopes, reef crests, and lagoon walls

decorPreferences: open space with good water circulation
mount cup forms upright so the cup faces upward

diet.notes: The Turbinaria Coral obtains most of its nutrition through photosynthesis carried out by zooxanthellae — symbiotic algae living within the coral's tissue. Within its family (Dendrophylliidae), Turbinaria is one of only three genera known to harbor zooxanthellae, making it more dependent on light than many of its close relatives. Most species extend their polyps at night to feed, though T. peltata is notable for tending to expand its polyps during the day instead. Weekly supplemental target-feeding with small meaty foods such as brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or marine plankton has been reported to support growth and coloration.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: corals placed in direct contact (growth can cause physical stress to neighbors)
aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles (e.g. Hammer, Torch, Galaxea)
fish known to nip at coral polyps

imageCandidates.notes: Tim Sheerman-Chase, 3307x2480. Turbinaria mesenterina: vivid yellow plating colony in-situ on Red Sea reef with anthias around it; sharp, whole colony in frame, most striking colour of the set.
Stan Shebs, 1800x1146. Turbinaria reniformis: vivid lime/yellow-green ruffled scroll plates with the diagnostic yellow rim; sharp, shows the classic scroll growth form.
Ryan McMinds, 2876x3000. Turbinaria stellulata: yellow star-polyp encrusting colony in-situ in blue water; sharp, whole colony, different species for genus breadth.
Christian Gloor, 1536x2048. Turbinaria mesenterina: large layered plating colony in a full reef scene; tack sharp, natural tan colour, light corner watermark.
Diego Delso, 5792x5792. Turbinaria mesenterina: single clean cup/vase form with white-dotted polyps on sand; very sharp, isolated subject, shows the cup growth form.
Diego Delso, 3083x4625. Turbinaria stellulata: crisp vivid macro of the neon-yellow stellate polyps; feature close-up kept as non-recommended extra (does not show whole colony).

Chaloklum Diving, 1000x725. Turbinaria peltata (the hobby 'Cup/Pagoda Coral'): two in-situ disc colonies, one with polyps extended; sharp coral detail, muted brown wild colour, added for genus breadth.

summary: Turbinaria is a genus of large polyp stony (LPS) corals found across the Indo-Pacific, prized for their striking cup, scroll, and plate growth forms. Generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly reef corals, tolerating moderate lighting and flow with a peaceful temperament.
careNotes:
Three primary growth forms are encountered in the hobby:
• Cup / Vase form (T. peltata): Forms a deep cup or vase shape, typically yellow to cream. The cup orientation matters — mount upright so the calyx faces upward to allow food and detritus to settle toward the polyps. T. peltata tends to have its polyps extended during the day (unusual for the genus).
• Scroll / Ruffled form (T. reniformis): Forms layered, folded scroll-like plates with ruffled edges. Often yellow-green to lime green. Generally grows outward horizontally.
• Plating form (T. mesenterina): Grows as flat to gently curved plates, sometimes tiered. Commonly sold as 'Pagoda Coral' in the trade.

Turbinaria are among the more beginner-accessible LPS corals and are generally considered hardy. Key care points:
• Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally advised (approximately 75–200 PAR). They have been reported to adapt to a range of intensities — lower than many SPS corals. Acclimate new specimens by starting lower in the tank and gradually moving them to their final position over 1–2 weeks.
• Flow: Moderate, indirect water movement is generally recommended. Sufficient flow to keep the coral surface free of detritus without direct blasting, which can stress the tissue.
• Placement: Bottom to middle of the aquarium is most commonly recommended, though they tend to adapt to a range of positions. Cup-form morphs should be mounted upright. The cup or plate surfaces can accumulate detritus — moderate flow helps prevent this.
• Feeding: Mostly dependent on photosynthesis via zooxanthellae. Most species are nocturnal — polyps expand at night — so target-feeding in the evening or after lights-out tends to be more effective. Weekly supplemental feeding with small meaty foods is generally considered beneficial.
• Growth and spacing: Turbinaria can grow quickly under good conditions and may reach colony sizes of 30–60 cm over years in a home aquarium. Adequate spacing from neighboring corals is advisable to prevent physical crowding as colonies expand.
• Low aggression: Turbinaria are generally described as peaceful reef inhabitants that do not produce long sweeper tentacles or aggressive allelopathic exudates. However, physical contact from expanding colonies can still stress neighbors, so space accordingly.
• Seldom targeted by crown-of-thorns starfish in the wild, which may reflect mild natural defenses.
• Standard reef supplementation of calcium, magnesium, alkalinity, and trace elements is generally advised as for any stony coral.
breedingNotes: Fragging (physical division) is possible but less commonly practiced than with branching LPS corals; the plate and cup forms are more difficult to frag cleanly without band saws or similar tools. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs. Captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Cup Coral, Pagoda (Turbinaria sp.)
primary.notes: Primary source. Two LiveAquaria product pages consulted (Cup Coral Pagoda and Australian Green Cup Coral Turbinaria and Yellow Cup Coral Turbinaria) — all confirmed same core parameters: temperature 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, flow Medium, lighting Moderate. Care level listed as Easy on 2 of 3 pages, Moderate on 1.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Turbinaria (multiple product listings)
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. WWC product pages consistently list Light: Medium, Flow: Medium, Care: Easy — confirming LiveAquaria lighting/flow/care designations. WWC does not provide numeric water parameter values on product pages.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Turbinaria (coral)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Dendrophylliidae, order Scleractinia), species list (17 species including T. peltata, T. reniformis, T. mesenterina), native range (Red Sea, Indian Ocean, Japan, south Central Pacific), and morphological descriptions. Confirmed zooxanthellate status. Not used for husbandry parameters.
